data.title: Banks Finance 750 Million Debt Package For 1.5 Billion Eat Happy--Hana Deal
data.description:
Wall Street's financing engine is showing signs of reopening, but the execution still looks selective. Banks including Deutsche Bank (DOD), RBC Capital Markets, and UniCredit are backing a 750 million debt package to support the roughly 1.5 billion combination of Eat Happy Group and Hana Group's European operations, according to people familiar with the matter. The structure includes a 650 million term loan alongside a 100 million revolving credit facility, giving the combined platform liquidity as it integrates and expands. One Rock Capital Partners is also stepping in with an equity investment, although terms tied to that contribution were not disclosed.

What's notable here is the flexibility embedded in the exit path for lenders. The underwriting group is considering distributing the debt either as leveraged loans or high-yield bonds, depending on how market conditions evolve. That decision could be critical, particularly as recent syndications have required pricing concessions to clear, reflecting investor caution tied to geopolitical tensions and concerns around broader business disruption from artificial intelligence. In that context, structuring optionality upfront may help banks manage risk while still supporting deal flow.

More broadly, this transaction sits within a reopening pipeline of acquisition-related financings that is beginning to build momentum again. Banks have sold roughly $35 billion of similar deals over the past three weeks, including large transactions tied to Electronic Arts (EA) and Sealed Air . At the same time, activity in Europe remains active, with lenders preparing an approximate 4 billion financing to support Carlyle's acquisition of BASF's (BASFY) coatings unit. Taken together, the setup suggests deal activity could be stabilizing, although pricing discipline and investor demand may continue to shape how these transactions ultimately get placed.

data.title: Latin America Less Exposed to Crude Supply Disruptions — Market Talk
data.description: Latin American countries are relatively shielded from energy supply disruptions caused by the war in Iran, Oxford Economics' Mauricio Monge writes. The region is less dependent on hydrocarbon imports from the Middle East and "uninterrupted fuel imports from the US shielded Latin America from the major physical supply shocks seen in Asia," Monge says. He adds that Argentina, Brazil and Guyana are expanding oil and gas production, while U.S. intervention "will likely push Venezuela's oil production close to its pre-crisis peak levels." Monge notes that Mexico "is the only oil-producing country in the region with an energy trade deficit, joining non-oil producers like Chile and Peru." (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

data.title: Goldman Says $240 Billion Selloff Could Flip to $55 Billion Buying
data.description:
The forced unwind may be nearing exhaustion, and positioning now looks like it could be shifting at the margin. After systematic strategies drove a sharp reduction in equity exposure during the recent selloff, flows are starting to stabilize, suggesting the mechanical drag that weighed on markets could begin to ease if price action holds.

According to Goldman Sachs' trading desk, fast-money investors including commodity trading advisers and volatility-targeting strategies sold roughly $240 billion of global equities over the past month as markets reacted to a decline tied in part to higher oil prices and geopolitical tensions linked to the war in Iran. That selling pressure appears to be moderating, with Goldman estimating these strategies could shift to net buying of about $55 billion over the next month, including roughly $20 billion in US equities. The ramp is expected to be measured, with only around $5 billion of buying projected over the coming week, implying the near-term bid may build gradually rather than provide immediate support.

The path from here likely depends on market direction. Goldman's models suggest that if the S&P 500 Index (SPY) were to gain around 8% over the next month, systematic strategies could scale into as much as $220 billion of global equity purchases, with more than half allocated to US markets. In contrast, a further decline of roughly 10% could trigger an additional $110 billion of selling. On the technical side, the 6,720 to 6,740 range is flagged as a key re-engagement zone where short- and medium-term trend signals could turn positive, potentially accelerating inflows if momentum improves.

data.title: Fed's Williams: FOMC leadership not in question amid slow Warsh confirmation
data.description:
Key points:
  • Fed's Williams says no uncertainty around FOMC leadership
  • Williams indicates Chair Powell would lead FOMC until Warsh confirmed
  • Little clarity when chair nominee Warsh will be confirmed

By Michael S. Derby

Federal Reserve Bank of New York President John Williams said on Tuesday that U.S. Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell would remain in charge of the central bank’s key monetary policy function even if Kevin Warsh has not been confirmed as the new Fed leader by next month.

"There's no issue of continuity. There's no issue of anything” when it comes to the leadership of the central bank’s interest-rate-setting Federal Open Market Committee, Williams said on Bloomberg’s television channel.

Williams was commenting on the unsettled succession process for Fed leadership. U.S. President Donald Trump this year named Warsh, a former Fed governor once known for his hawkish views on monetary policy, to succeed Powell when his term as leader concludes in May.

INVESTIGATION COMPLICATES TRANSITION

There has been little formal movement to get Warsh in place to take over from Powell, which means time for a seamless transition is running out. The outlook is further complicated by a legal investigation the Department of Justice has directed at the Fed and Powell.

That widely criticized investigation into cost overruns for Fed headquarters renovations has driven a key Senate Republican to say Warsh will not be confirmed until the probe is resolved. With the Trump administration vowing to press forward regardless, the investigation has increased the odds Warsh will not be confirmed by the time Powell’s term as chair ends.

Powell has said he will remain chair until a new leader is confirmed, a position that is grounded in central bank history. "If my successor is not confirmed by the end of my term as chair, I would serve as chair pro tem,” Powell said after the mid-March FOMC meeting.

Powell, who also holds a Fed governor term that extends until 2028, said he would serve out the governor role “until the investigation is well and truly over with transparency and finality.”

DUAL ROLE

Further complicating the situation is the fact that while the Fed chair and the FOMC chair have always been the same person, the FOMC chair is technically voted on by central bank officials. For some, that has created uncertainty as to who will serve as the key voice setting interest rate policy.

“Typically, we have this vote in January for the chair of the FOMC and, or if, a new chair is confirmed by the Senate, then that person becomes the chair of the FOMC,” Williams said. The New York Fed president serves as vice chair of the FOMC and runs the bank responsible for implementing monetary policy.

“The most important thing here is that … we're just focused on doing our work,” Williams said.

The central banker had some favorable words for Warsh, as some observers worry the chair nominee could be unusually deferential to a president who has pressured the central bank for lower interest rates.

Warsh “understands the Federal Reserve very well” as a former central banker, and “he has a keen understanding of what our mission is, and the importance of what we do,” Williams said.

“I haven't spoken to him lately, but I do expect that when he does get confirmed by the Senate, that he will share his views and perspectives as he thinks about … what he wants to accomplish as chair,” he said.

data.title: Tesla Stock Falls Again -- JPMorgan Says Avoid Tesla at all Costs
data.description:
Tesla shares continue to slide after JPMorgan reaffirmed its Underweight rating on the electric vehicle maker.

After Monday's slump, Tesla shares fell more than 3% in Tuesday morning trading as well.

Analyst Ryan Brinkman pointed to weaker-than-expected first-quarter delivery numbers and a year-over-year decline in energy storage installations. The team now expects Tesla to report quarterly earnings per share of $0.30, down from a prior estimate of $0.43 and below the consensus forecast of $0.38, the note showed.

Brinkman said the firm sees roughly 60% downside to its $145 price target for December 2026. He advised investors to approach the stock with caution, citing execution risks and valuation concerns, according to the note.

The analyst noted that Tesla shares trade more than 50% higher than when delivery volume peaked in June 2022, despite sluggish growth in vehicle deliveries. JPMorgan said the company's differentiated business model and technology may be offset by rising competition, brand controversy, and valuation that appears to price in substantial future growth.

data.title: EIA Raises Oil-Price Forecasts as War in Middle East Continues
data.description:
By Anthony Harrup

The Energy Information Administration raised its forecasts for crude oil prices in 2026 as the prolonged closure of the Strait of Hormuz shuts in production in the Middle East and wipes out its expected global inventory builds.

In its latest Short Term Energy Outlook, the EIA raised its 2026 forecast for Brent crude to $96 a barrel from $79 a barrel previously. It now expects U.S. benchmark West Texas Intermediate crude to average $87 a barrel, up from $74 a barrel in the March outlook.

The EIA estimates that Iraq, Saudi Arabia, Kuwait, the United Arab Emirates, Qatar and Bahrain collectively shut in 7.5 million barrels a day of crude oil production in March, and expects that to rise to 9.1 million barrels a day in April.

"We assume the conflict does not persist past April and that traffic through the Strait of Hormuz gradually resumes," the agency said Tuesday, adding that shut-ins are expected to fall to 6.7 million barrels a day in May and return to near prewar levels in late 2026.

The EIA now expects global oil inventories to draw down by 300,000 barrels a day on average this year, compared with its previous expectation of a 1.9 million barrels a day increase. It also cut its 2026 oil-demand-growth forecast by half, to 600,000 barrels a day, with a rebound to 1.6 million barrels a day in 2027.

The agency sees Brent peaking at an average $115 a barrel in the second quarter, falling below $90 in the fourth quarter, and averaging $76 a barrel in 2027. The forecast depends greatly on the duration of the conflict and resulting production outages, the EIA said.

On the domestic side, the EIA expects average U.S. gasoline prices at the pump to peak around $4.30 a gallon in April, and average more than $3.70 in all of 2026. Diesel prices are seen above $5.80 a gallon in April and $4.80 a gallon in 2026.

Write to Anthony Harrup at anthony.harrup@wsj.com

data.title: US power use to beat record highs in 2026 and 2027 as AI use surges, EIA says
data.description:
Key points:
  • Rising power demand attributed to AI and crypto data centers, electrification trends
  • Renewables' share of power generation to increase, coal's share to decline, EIA forecasts
  • Natural gas use for power generation to remain high, residential and commercial gas sales to fall

By Scott DiSavino

U.S. power consumption, which hit its second straight annual record high in 2025, will rise further in 2026 and 2027, the Energy Information Administration said in its Short-Term Energy Outlook on Tuesday.

The EIA projected power demand will rise from a record 4,195 billion kilowatt-hours (kWh) in 2025 to 4,244 billion kWh in 2026 and 4,381 billion kWh in 2027.

Demand is surging due in large part to data centers dedicated to artificial intelligence and cryptocurrency, and as homes and businesses use more electricity and less fossil fuels for heat and transportation.

The EIA forecasts power sales in 2026 will rise to 1,520 billion kWh for residential consumers, 1,528 billion kWh for commercial customers and 1,053 billion kWh for industrial customers.

Those forecasts compare with all-time highs of 1,515 billion kWh for residential consumers in 2025, 1,493 billion kWh in 2025 for commercial customers and 1,064 billion kWh for industrial customers in 2000.

As renewable output rises, the EIA said the share of power generation from coal will slide from 17% in 2025 to 16% in 2026 and 15% in 2027. Natural gas' share would ease from 40% in 2025 to 39% in 2026 before rising back to 40% in 2027.

The percentage of renewable generation will rise from around 24% in 2025 to 25% in 2026 and 27% in 2027, while nuclear power's share will hold at 18% in 2025, 2026, and 2027, according to the outlook.

The EIA projected gas sales in 2026 would slide to 12.6 billion cubic feet per day (bcfd) for residential consumers, 9.4 bcfd for commercial customers and 23.2 bcfd for industrial customers, but rise to 36.1 bcfd for power generation.

Those figures compare with all-time highs of 14.3 bcfd in 1996 for residential consumers, 9.9 bcfd in 2025 for commercial customers, 23.8 bcfd in 1973 for industrial customers, and 36.8 bcfd in 2024 for power generation.

data.title: Tartisan Nickel Corp. Passes All Matters at Annual General Meeting of Shareholders
data.description:
Toronto, Ontario--(Newsfile Corp. - April 7, 2026) - Tartisan Nickel Corp. (OTCQX: TTSRF) (FSE: 8TA) ("Tartisan", or the "Company") is pleased to report that all matters passed at the Annual General Meeting of Shareholders held today.

At the meeting, the three existing directors were appointed with 99.998% votes cast in favour, namely D. Mark Appleby, Carl J. McGill and Yves P. Clement.

Mark Appleby will continue in his role as President and CEO and Omar Gonzalez, CPA has been appointed as the Chief Financial Officer.

Clearhouse LLP have been appointed as the Company's auditors and the Company's Stock Option Plan was approved.

CEO Mark Appleby said, "We would like to thank shareholders for their continued support as we ramp up the Kenbridge Nickel-Copper-Cobalt Project and prepare for Greenfields work at our Sill Lake and Turtle Pond Projects".

About Tartisan Nickel Corp.

Tartisan Nickel Corp. is a Canadian-based critical minerals exploration and development company which owns, the Kenbridge Nickel Project near Sioux Narrows, Northwestern Ontario, the Sill Lake Silver Property near Sault Ste. Marie, Ontario as well as the Night Danger Turtle Pond project near Dryden, Ontario.

Tartisan Nickel Corp. common shares are listed on the Canadian Securities Exchange (OTCQX: TTSRF) (FSE: 8TA). Currently, there are 155,005,114 shares outstanding (159,234,724 fully diluted).

For further information, please contact Mark Appleby, President & CEO, and a Director of the Company, at 416-804-0280 (info@tartisannickel.com). Additional information about Tartisan Nickel Corp. can be found at the Company's website at www.tartisannickel.com or on SEDAR+ at www.sedarplus.ca

This news release may contain forward-looking statements including but not limited to comments regarding the timing and content of upcoming work programs, geological interpretations, receipt of property titles, potential mineral recovery processes, etc. Forward-looking statements address future events and conditions and therefore involve inherent risks and uncertainties. Actual results may differ materially from those currently anticipated in such statements.

The Canadian Securities Exchange (operated by CNSX Markets Inc.) has neither approved nor disapproved of the contents of this press release.

data.title: Wednesday preview: Delta Air Lines results, RBNZ policy announcement due
data.description:
In the US, earnings from Constellation Brands and Delta Air Lines will be in focus, along with the latest minutes from the Federal Open Market Committee.

Michael Brown, senior research strategist at Pepperstone, said the minutes from the March FOMC meeting "are likely too stale to offer much by way of fresh guidance".

In Europe, German factory orders for February will be released, along with February eurozone retail sales figures and the HCOB eurozone construction PMI for March.

Also in focus will be the latest policy announcement from the RBNZ, which is widely expected to leave rates unchanged at 2.25%.

"Overnight, we expect the Reserve Bank of New Zealand (RBNZ) to keep its monetary policy unchanged in line with market pricing and consensus," said Danske Bank.

"Markets expect RBNZ to hike rates most likely in Q3 this year."

ING’s Chris Turner said that as far as the RBNZ is concerned, very little tightening is priced in the first half of the year, with 65 basis points of hikes priced by the end of the year.

"RBNZ Governor Anna Breman has proved quite dovish in her communication so far, and in the absence of any new RBNZ forecasts tomorrow, consensus widely expects unchanged rates at 2.25% and probably little steer on future tightening," he said.

"Lacking the industrial commodities offered by Australia, the New Zealand dollar has substantially underperformed the Australian dollar this year. Barring a surprise hawkish shift at tomorrow's RBNZ press conference, this trend may well continue."

data.title: The Putting Stance That Can Be More Fun for Golfers — but Hard on the Ego — Journal Report
data.description:
By Laz Versalles

The reason golfers should try the face-on putting technique, also known as sidesaddle, is likely the same reason they typically won't: optics.

Face-on putting positions the golfer behind and just to the side of the ball, allowing the golfer to use both eyes to look down the target line. This approach gives the player far better optics than the traditional stance at the side of the ball.

The trade-off — and this is a big deal for a lot of people — is style points. The optics of standing behind the ball in such a stance are...not great.

"I'd get double takes, some laughs, some snickering, I'd get all of it," says Doug Wilton, a former collegiate golfer who has sidesaddled for well over a decade. "Then you start making some long ones, and suddenly everyone wants to try your putter."

Most players discover face-on putting because they have struggled with traditional putting. Face-on putting brings many of them joy and results. Most of them will tell you that not only is it fun, it's logical.

If you're still not convinced, think of it this way: When you're bowling, throwing darts or shooting pool — all endeavors with an emphasis on targets — you're in a face-on position. So it only makes sense to do so when putting as well.

As Ben Hogan said...

Nine-time major champion Ben Hogan wasn't a fan of putting, especially late in his career. Hogan once said, "I have always contended golf is one game and putting is another." He continued, "One game is played in the air, the other is played on the ground."

The golf we play in the air — the drives, the full shots, the pitching — require the player to swing and produce speed with the club to get the ball airborne. To do that, we stand at the side of the ball to swing our club on a circular plane, generate some force, and hopefully make solid contact so that the energy from the swing produces a shot that takes flight.

The game of putting, as Hogan put it, is just rolling a ball along a line at a certain pace to finish in or near the target. That's all it is. We don't need to generate much speed or force, so we really don't need to take a traditional golf stance.

Dr. Craig Farnsworth, an optometrist who has worked with golf professionals, explains the advantages of face-on putting: "When you're over the ball in a traditional putting stance it is a monocular situation, which means you're really just using one eye. When a player is in a face-on position, they are binocular, using both eyes, which gives them a better chance at getting the distance or pace right."

The nonconformists

Just about every golf club has a nonconformist in its ranks who sidesaddles. Randy Haag, who has won the Northern California Player of the Year title 11 times, as well as club championships at the prestigious Olympic Club, is probably the most accomplished face-on putter in American history. "I went from the putter being the worst club in my bag to the best club in my bag," he says. "I've probably won 200 tournaments putting sidesaddle."

Haag stopped using sidesaddle 2 1/2 years ago because he felt at that point he would be more effective with a traditional stance from close range.

In the professional ranks, Sam Snead, who is tied with Tiger Woods for the most PGA Tour wins (82), is the best-known face-on putter. Snead started to use the stance as he battled the yips — an affliction I'm all-too-familiar with. His croquet approach (where you straddle the ball, as opposed to face-on, where both feet are on the same side of the ball) was so successful that the U.S. Golf Association made it illegal to straddle the ball in 1968.

More recently, Bryson DeChambeau tried the face-on technique on the PGA Tour. He turned away from it after a few months in early 2017, in part because the putter he was using as part of the experiment was deemed illegal by the U.S. Golf Association, according to interviews he gave at the time.

As someone who has flirted on and off with the technique for years, I can say that it's a lot more fun than traditional putting. It's easy to learn, and lets me use a different club now and then specifically designed for face-on putting (I'm a sucker for novelty). And maybe it's in my head, but I feel more confident hitting longer putts face-on.

Do I look silly? It wouldn't be the first time. But any embarrassment lasts about as long as it takes to sink a putt.

Laz Versalles is a writer and golfer in Santa Monica, Calif. He can be reached at reports@wsj.com.

data.title: Cybersecurity Funding Doubles From Previous Year in 1Q — Market Talk
data.description: Cybersecurity funding more than doubled to $4.62 billion in 1Q, according to a report from Pinpoint Search Group. Pinpoint says the quarter was one of the strongest since the 2021 to 2022 peak cycle. "The data reflects a market that has moved beyond the correction phase seen in recent years, with capital returning in a more structured manner," Pinpoint says. The report finds that early-stage funding made up the majority of transaction volume, but those rounds represented a smaller share of the capital. Much of the capital raised was concentrated among multiple funding rounds exceeding $100 million, according to the report. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

data.title: Delta Air Lines Hikes Bag Fee as Industry Tries to Offset Surging Fuel Costs — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Kit Norton

Delta Air Lines is the latest carrier to raise its fees for checked luggage as the industry grapples with rising jet fuel prices brought on by the Iran war.

Delta said Tuesday it will increase checked luggage fees by $10 to $45 for a first item and $55 for a second item. The fee for a third checked bag will go up $50 to $200. The new prices take effect for tickets purchased starting Wednesday.

Delta didn't immediately respond to request for comment from Barron's.

United Airlines and JetBlue Airways made similar moves last week, as spillover effects from the Iran war have pushed up energy prices and pressured airlines' profit margins.

Price hikes are bad for travelers, but the recent moves may suggest even more trouble for investors. Airline stocks have broadly suffered from a spike in jet fuel prices during the Iran war. Higher jet fuel prices mean it is incredibly difficult for airlines to generate profits, according to analysts.

Delta stock declined 1.4% to $65.80 on Tuesday. United Airlines shares fell 2.2% to $88.69 and JetBlue dropped 2.4% to $4.47.

Delta Air Lines reports first-quarter earnings before the opening bell Wednesday. Wall Street forecasts earnings of 57 cents per share, compared to 46 cents per share a year ago, with revenue up 9% to $1.45 billion, according to FactSet.

Write to Kit Norton at kit.norton@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Travel Agencies' AI Tools Seen as Slow to Catch on with Consumers — Market Talk
data.description: Agentic AI travel booking is likely to have a bigger impact on corporate travel than leisure at first, D.A. Davidson analysts say. Booking's Kayak brand recently launched an AI tool designed to act as a travel assistant and planner. Consumers who are booking leisure trips tend to feel there is more at stake when planning travel and are often afraid to book a flight on their phones, whereas corporate customers know exactly what they want, the analysts say. "It's not really clear to the Kayak team when travelers will be ready to book a leisure trip/vacation fully agentic," the analysts say. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

data.title: Sovato CEO Sees Minimal Competitive Risk — Market Talk
data.description: Sovato's new CEO Brian Miller says the remote surgery startup's platform faces scant competition. Sovato's platform provides the digital infrastructure that allows doctors to remotely perform surgery and diagnostics using robots, including secure network links to ensure connectivity as well as an intelligent layer that helps manage clinical workflows and logistics, he says. "As you look at those pieces of the puzzle, there are some things out there in other companies, but I don't know of any other that is digging in," he tells WSJ. He says Sovato has a competitive edge because it is working to deeply integrate into how hospitals operate to ensure its customers get the most value out of the platform. "To date, I haven't seen anything else out there like that," he says. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

data.title: Goldman Private Credit Sees 4.999% Redemptions, Stays Below 5% Cap
data.description:
Investor pressure is building across private credit, but Goldman Sachs may be holding its ground better than most. In the first quarter, the firm's $15.7 billion Private Credit Corp. reported redemption requests of 4.999% of outstanding shares, coming in just below the industry's standard 5% quarterly cap. That compares with peers that have faced significantly heavier withdrawal demands, in some cases forcing them to limit investor exits. While redemption activity increased from 3.5% in the fourth quarter, Goldman still managed to meet all requests, suggesting a more stable investor base relative to others in the space.

A key differentiator appears to be who is providing the capital. Goldman pointed to its institutional orientation, which could be proving more resilient as retail investors pull back amid concerns around lending standards and exposure to companies vulnerable to artificial intelligence disruption. The fund also highlighted positive net flows of approximately $1.04 billion during the quarter, contrasting with negative flows reported by many competitors. Management emphasized that while the fund operates in the same environment as peers, its diversified capital base and origination platform could allow it to be more patient and selective in deploying capital through the cycle.

At the same time, performance trends suggest the broader asset class is under pressure. Goldman's fund generated a 0.4% return through the end of February, down from 1.3% in the same period last year, while peers have also reported weaker results, including a monthly loss at an Ares Management fund. Still, Goldman indicated that reduced capital inflows into private credit could be creating more attractive conditions, with wider spreads and stronger documentation emerging as lenders reliant on retail funding begin to step back. That shift could point to a more disciplined lending environment, even as near-term demand dynamics remain uneven.
